title: Short-horizon sequence sidecar over last 3-6 laps
summary: Recent pit-stop papers report that temporal models, especially Bi-LSTM-style architectures, outperform simpler deep baselines because pit calls depend on developing trends in degradation and pace, not one-row snapshots.
rationale: Recent pit-stop papers report that temporal models, especially Bi-LSTM-style architectures, outperform simpler deep baselines because pit calls depend on developing trends in degradation and pace, not one-row snapshots. The current best code uses only shallow lag features; a dedicated sequence sidecar is a distinct signal source.
implementation_hint: Build per-driver chronological windows of the last 3-6 laps using only past data. Start with a cheap version first: lag-stack the numeric columns and first differences for 1-5 lags into a LightGBM/CatBoost sidecar. If that helps, test a tiny TCN/GRU/BiLSTM on the same windows. Blend strictly with OOF predictions only.
expected_effect: Should sharpen detection of accelerating degradation or pace collapse just before stops, especially where single-lap deltas are noisy.
risk: Easy to overstate CV if windows cross folds or use future padding. Deep models may be slower and less robust than lag-stacked trees.
